---
title: "Module 9 Multivariate"
author: "Vashay Carpenter"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_documentt
---
First we will install our packages
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
#install.packages("pdftools") 
#install.packages("stringr") required to use str_split
#install.packages("ggplot2")
#install.packages("tidyverse")
#install.packages("corrgram")
library(stringr)
library(pdftools)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
library(corrgram)

```
Next we will get the data from the PDF file containing the 2024 Annual State Report of arrests for Missouri and format it into a csv
```{r cars}
#Let's create a var for the raw text from the pdf
raw_text <- pdf_text("C:/Users/cruos/Downloads/2024-Annual-State-Report-1.pdf")
#Create lines using unlist; str_split from the stringr package
lines <- unlist(str_split(raw_text, "\n"))

#Select the rows for each table starting using their line numbers; avoiding repeated column names and empty lines
table1 <- lines[29:48]
table2 <- lines[312:363]
table3 <- lines[374:397]

table1
#Combine the lines of each table
all_data <- c(table1, table2, table3)

# This creates the 8th column needed to stop the "Left Shift" in Excel.
all_data[1] <- paste0("Metric ", all_data[1])

#This var breaks the pattern utilized by all others by inserting a comma so we will replace it; avoiding duplicate row.names and comma induced errors
all_data <- str_replace_all(all_data, "Stop rate, residents", "Resident_stop_rate") 

#Replace all empty spaces with commas after removing the whitespace from the beginning and the ned of the document using str_trim
clean_csv <- str_replace_all(str_trim(all_data), "\\s{2,}", ",")

#Our final variable for creating our csv
clean_csv

#Create our final csv representing all scraped and cleaned data
writeLines(clean_csv, "STL_Enforcement_Master.csv")

```
Now we will transpose and plot the data available in our newlsy created csv
```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
#Read the newly created CSV back into R as a data frame
df_raw <- read.csv("STL_Enforcement_Master.csv", row.names = NULL)

#Assign standard racial and metric headers to the data frame
colnames(df_raw) <- c("Metric", "Total", "White", "Black", "Hispanic", "Native", "Asian", "Other")

#Filter for specific multivariate metrics: Stop, Search, Hit, and Arrest rates
metrics <- df_raw[df_raw$Metric %in% c("Stop rate", "Search rate", "Contraband hit rate", "Arrest rate"), ]

#Transpose the data frame so metrics are columns and races are rowst
df_plot <- as.data.frame(t(metrics[,-1]))

#Restore the metric names as column headers
colnames(df_plot) <- metrics$Metric

#Convert character values to numeric to enable mathematical plotting 
df_plot <- df_plot %>% 
  mutate(across(everything(), ~as.numeric(as.character(.))))

#Capture the race names from the row identifiers into a new column
df_plot$Race <- rownames(df_plot)

#Focus on demographic disparity
df_plot <- df_plot %>% filter(Race != "Total")

write.csv(df_plot, "Flipped_Enforcement_Data.csv", row.names = FALSE)


# Create plot: X=Search, Y=Hit Rate, Color=Race (Contrast), Size=Stop Rate (Hierarchy)
ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = `Search rate`, y = `Contraband hit rate`, color = Race, size = `Stop rate`)) +
  #Set point transparency to 0.8 to manage overlap (Balance)
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  #Place race labels near points for immediate identification (Proximity)
  geom_text(aes(label = Race), vjust = -1.5, fontface = "bold", size = 4, show.legend = FALSE) + 
  #Ensure the Y-axis has breathing room for labels (Balance)
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.2)))+
  #Use Set1 palette for maximum color differentiation (Contrast)
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + 
  #Clean title; subtitle removed for simplicity (Simplicity/Alignment)  
  labs(title = "Missouri Enforcement Paradox",
       x = "Search Rate (%)", y = "Contraband Hit Rate (%)") +
  #Use a minimal theme to remove visual clutter (Balance)
  theme_minimal() 


corrgram(df_plot[,1:4], order=TRUE, 
         lower.panel=panel.shade, 
         upper.panel=panel.pie, 
         text.panel=panel.txt,
         main="Multivariate Correlation: Enforcement Paradox")

```

Blog Link: https://sizzleyezation.blogspot.com/2026/03/module-9-assignment.html
